did.unittitle: Jones family archive

corpname.part: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

did.abstract: The collection comprises correspondence, photographs, sketchbooks, travel journals, notebooks, and printed material concerning the Jones family. This includes primary correspondent Caroline Jones (1814-1895), her son and photographer Henry Chapman Jones (1854-1922), and Henry's two sons - artist Sydney Langford (1888-1948) and banker Arthur Morley (1885-1964). The result is a collection that offers not only a window into the life of a large middle-class religious dissenting family in mid-nineteenth London, but one that documents the talent and skills of different family members working in various artistic disicplines into the twentieth century.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Frances Caroline Jones (nee Chapman) (1814-1895) was born to Thomas Chapman (1776-1846) and Jemima Langford (1777-1846). She attended boarding school in St. Omer France for a time, and made close friends there. Rather than be referred to by her first name Frances she went by her middle name, Caroline. Her family were a part of the Protestant Dissenters in England, a group that opposed the interference of the English government in matters of faith. They attended Poultry Chapel in London, one of the dissenting churches at the time. Caroline's religious upbringing would go on to impact her later descendants, especially during the first World War.

Caroline married Charles Jones (1813-1881) in 1837. They had nine children together, although not all survived into adulthood. Living in London meant that Caroline experienced the impact of the cholera epidemic and the impact it had on her children, as well as the overall dangers of childhood disease common at the time and the pressures of marriage and society. Her correspondence in the collection preserves her perspective on these topics.
Frances Caroline Jones died in February of 1895 in London.

Henry Chapman Jones (1854-1922) was a lecturer in chemistry at the Royal College of Science and a part of the expanding field of photography, writing widely regarding the technical parts of the process. He was born to Charles Jones and Frances Caroline Jones in St Olaves, London, England. His father, Charles, was a brass founder.

Little is currently known regarding his schooling, but Jones was an active and engaged chemist, serving as a lecturer on the subject at the Royal College of Science from 1881-1914. His obituary from the Journal of the Chemical Society in 1933 highlights his analytical chemistry work, as well Jones' work in photography. He published articles about the technical aspects of photography such as the density of negatives, factors that impact photographic plates, refractive powers, and color in various photography magazines and even lectured on the topic.

Henry married his wife Phoebe Simmons Morley (1885-1934) in July 1884. They had three children together - Sydney, Arthur, and Ernest - with Sydney and Arthur surviving into adulthood. Henry's interest in photography was an influence on his children, with Sydney in particular going on to work in the arts. Jones' obituary notes that he was well liked and considered a warm figure amongst his colleagues.
Henry Chapman Jones died on March 7, 1932 in Hanwell, and was buried in Ealing and Old Brentford Cemetery.

Sydney Langford Jones (1888-1948) was a sculptor, engraver, painter, and book illustrator known mostly for sculpture work on medals, such as the coronation medal of George VI and works for the Royal Horticultural Society, and wood engravings. He commonly signed himself S. Langford Jones and Jonah. He was born in Chelsea, and later moved with his family to Ealing. Sydney studied at the Royal College of Art, London, and graduated in 1911. Following graduation, he traveled in Italy with friend and jeweler Reginald Pearson, with whom he kept correspondence with until Pearson's death in World War One.

During the first World War, Sydney was among the conscientious objectors and cited his Quaker faith as the reason for his strong opposition to serving in the conflict. He was imprisoned for seventeen months at Pentonville prison for his refusal to serve. Although art supplies were forbidden in the prison, Jones used the porridge served to create sculptures and keep up his skills. Two surviving samples of his work are held by the Royal Berkshire Archives in the UK, along with two later paintings by Jones and his personal papers (call number D/EX1795).

He married his with Clara E. Billings (1882-1963) on March 26. 1929. Billings was a sculptor who trained at the Manchester School of Art, Royal College of Art, and then under Rodin in Paris. The two settled in Blewsbury and remained married until Clara's death in 1963. Sydney died one year later.

Arthur Morley Jones (1885-1964) was a banker. He was born to Henry Chapman Jones and Phoebe Simmons Morley on May 14, 1885. The family settled in Ealing. Little information is known regarding Arthur's schooling, but he left school in 1904 and became a banker at the London County and Westminster Bank.
He met fellow clerk Mary Houseman at the same bank, and Arthur proposed in 1912. The two wed in 1915 and went on to have two children, Katherine and Robert.

Like his brother Syndey, Arthur was a conscientious objector during World War One. Military documents held by the National Archives in the UK chart his own appeals from being conscripted into the armed forces, and shows that he missed his own hearing due to the time he was putting in at the bank.
Information regarding Arthur's life following the war is unknown. He died on March 8, 1964.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection comprises correspondence, photographs, sketchbooks, travel journals, notebooks, and printed material concerning Charles Jones (1780-1850) and his descendants. The correspondence spans the better part of the nineteenth century (1805-1878), with the bulk of the letters dating from the 1830s, 1840s, and 1850s; it offers a window into the life of a large middle-class dissenting family in mid nineteenth-century London.
The contents of the Jones family archive covers the years 1805 to 1945 in a variety of formats, creating a robust collection that documents the lives of three generations of the Jones family.

The first series, correspondence, covers the years 1805-1878 and include a number of letters written by young children of the Jones family; bundles of letters sent in the 1840s from St Omer (France) to Frances Caroline Chapman.

Henry Chapman Jones's material in series II provides some chronological overlap with the first series in the form of his handwriting book and notebook of poetry written in his youth. The rest of the material focuses on his research and publishing o the rapidly expanding field of photography.

Series III. contains material created by Syndey Langford Jones, including school notebooks from both his childhood schooling and time at the Royal College of Art. There are also undated sketchbooks and a copy of A Peace Portfolio , which was published in 1935.
Arthur Morley's material in series IV. consists primarily of photograph albums of family holidays in the early 1910s. It also contains a sketchbook from 1945.

Additional material created by other members of the Jones family have been placed in Series V, either because the creator has only one item in the collection or the exact owner of the item is unknown. This series includes the travel diary of Mary Ann Jones during her time in Germany in 1893 to further her musical and cultural education, and a recipe book signed only "Mrs. Chapman Jones" but most likely Henry's wife, Phoebe. Several almanacs are also included, most with inscriptions.

Photographs have been placed in series six, with the vast majority of the material coming from a photograph album tha thas since been unbound. In addition to photography, it includes hand-made Christmas and birthday cards by family members- especially by Sydney Langford and Arthur Morley; a 1908 menu card "du diner des Savants," signed by Major Baden Powell and Ernest Shackleton; and woodcuts likely made by Sydney.
Realia has been placed at the end of the collection in series VII. It includes a portrait of Charles Jones, as well as a key, a leather coin purse with change, and several cufflinks.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The collection is the physical property of the Yale Center for British Art. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the Archives Department.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Jones family archive. Yale Center for British Art, Paul Mellon Fund

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Yale Center for British Art, Paul Mellon Fund

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged into the following seven series: Series I. Correspondence; Series II. Henry Chapman Jones; Series III. Sydney Langford Jones; Series IV. Arthur Morley Jones; Series V. Other Jones family members; Series VI. Photographs; Series VII. Realia.

did.unittitle: Series I. Correspondence

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Jones correspondence spans the better part of the nineteenth century (1805-1878) with the bulk of the letters dating from the 1830s, 1840s, and 1850s and offering a window into the life of a large middle class dissenting family in mid-19th century London.

Charles Jones was a brass founder, and several of the letters give a glimpse into the workings of his business and the lives of the men and boys he employed (including his son Charles) in his small shop. The Jones family worshipped at the dissenting Poultry Chapel in the city and buried their dead at Abney Park, the cemetery of choice for dissenters after Bunhill Fields had filled up. The collection has particular usefulness not only for social historians interested in Victorian middle classes, but also for religious historians, historians, of gender, and historians of the family – especially historians of childhood.

Much of the early material in the Jones archive relates to Henry Chapman Jone's mother: Caroline Chapman, and later Caroline Jones. Specifically, it offers a glimpse of her protracted and emotionally intense relationship with a friend from the boarding school in St. Omer, France, where Caroline had spent several years as a teenager. The relationship between the two young women is in many ways characteristic of the "female world of love and ritual" described by the historian Caroll Smith-Rosenberg: a world of emotionally intense bonds between women, separate from but fully compatible with heterosexual and heterosocial world of familial and matrimonial life. The Jones archive offers an opportunity to intervene in the debates sparkled by Smith-Rosenburg's work: both by raising intriguing questions about the ambivalent place of female friendship in the worlds of school, work, and family, and by offering a context in which to explore these questions that transcends the strictly Anglo-American framework in which they are usually examined.

Much of the correspondence dating from the later 1840s and 1850s involves the Jones children, especially the two eldest sons Charles and Thomas, both of whom died young. Thomas, the younger of the two, spent a series of stints with friends and relatives in the countryside, recuperating from various illnesses, and his absences provided the occasion for much of the correspondence. The letters detail not only the progress of his own symptoms, but also his religious education and development, and his daily life and activities. In the summer and fall of 1854, the letters include many references to the cholera epidemic that was then ravaging London. Several of the Jones children became ill during this period, perhaps with cholera, and Charles died in early November from what the family doctor described as "simple fever." The 1854 letters between Thomas and Charles, in particular, offer a window into the history of childhood in mid-Victorian England, from the perspective of the children themselves. In addition, these letters provide a glimpse of the social experience of epidemic disease and death in the nineteenth century.

The later material includes a number of letters penned by the younger Jones children, as well as letters from Caroline Jones to her husband, which include descriptions of and reflections upon the social, religious, and practical dimensions of a Victorian marriage.
Caroll Smith-Rosenberg, "The Female World of Love and Ritual: Relations between Women in Nineteenth Century America," Signs 1 (1975)

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Chapman to James Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Advises his brother James that he is sending him clothing, admonishes him for not writing, and urges him to be "not unmindfull of Religion."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Chapman to James Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Acknowledges receipt of James's letter, admonishes him to write more often and to "learn to live the life of a child of God."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Chapman to James Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes a family dispute, perhaps during James's visit to London.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquire to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss Caroline Chapman, No. 20 Bucklesbury, Cheapside, London, England." Pulchérie writes mournfully of Caroline's departure from St. Omer [France], where she had been "en pension." In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Chapman to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports briefly on the health of relatives; informs Caroline that [her sister] Emma has sent two collars and can send more. Mentions that he sent "the [letter] for St. Omer by Post last evening."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Chapman to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports on illnesses of friends and family and news from Caroline's sisters. Mentions his "anxiety to see you at home again," and remarks that he often thinks "it like a dream when I reflect how we as a Famely (sic) are situated and separated (sic)." Mentions that he intends to write to "poor Jane" and encourages Caroline to do the same, as she is "shut up or we may say cut off from all Gospel means of spiritual improvement."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Chapman to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to Caroline Chapman at Pickford House, near East Grinstead, Sussex. Mentions that he is "glad you have wrote to Jane. I have read it and like it very much." Offers to send her "any of our periodicals… either the Evan[gelist] Mag[azine] the youth or the revivalist."

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss Caroline Chapman, 20 Bucklesbury, Cheapside, London, England." Complains that she has not received a letter from Caroline in five months, and worries that Caroline might be ill, that she might be trying to break off their correspondence, or that she might have taken offense at something Pulchérie had said in a previous letter. Assures Caroline that she is no longer friends with Susanna Leigh; she has decided that Susannah is inconstant and deceitful.
Mentions that she risks incurring the disfavor of one of the mistresses; also worries that "Mlle. Rosalie" has not been sending off her letters. In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from E[mma] J. Chapman to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Emma writes from Basingstoke [Hants]. Describes the death of their aunt. Reports that their aunt, Mrs. Charles Chapman, had stated before her death that she "had reason to bless God that she had a husband who was the means of inducing her to attend the house of God, she was quite opposed to any thing serious when first she was married."
Includes note from Thomas Chapman.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss C. Chapman, 20 Bucklesbury, Cheapside, London," but this address has been crossed out and replaced (in a different hand) with "Miss Chapman, Edger Esqr., Pickstone, near East Grinstead, Sussex."

Pulchérie expresses excitement at the prospect of seeing Caroline at St. Omer soon: "deja je compte les heures, les instantes; ce jour si longtemps attendu arrive dans 137 jours." Passes along news of Caroline's sister, Jane, and other happenings at the pension. Notes that "les maitresses sont si difficile cette année que peu de choses peuvent échapper à leur surveillance." In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss C. Chapman, 20 Bucklesbury, Cheapside, London."

Pulchérie explains that "Mlle Rosalie" has been keeping her too busy to write, but that today claimed to have had a headache and stayed in while the others went for a walk. Anticipates with excitement Caroline's upcoming visit, even as she mournfully contemplates the inevitability of Caroline's eventual departure and the possibility that they will never see each other again. Reports that Caroline's sister Jane has been successful in her studies, and passes along other news of happenings in St. Omer, including a music competition to take place among inhabitants of Dunkirk, Bergues, Graveline, and Aire. Wonders if Caroline shares her opinion of the Royal Family, and hopes that she supports Charles X, and not "l'usurpateur impudent."

Pulchérie speculates that she has not received a letter from Caroline that month because Caroline's "occupation" has kept her from writing. Caroline, it seems, is a governess. In the course of recounting news about girls from the pension who have gotten places as governesses, Pulchérie speculates that Caroline is ideally suited for her post. In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss C. Chapman, 20 Bucklesbury, Cheapside, London, England," which has been struck out and replaced with "Miss Chapman, Sr Edger Esqr, Pickstone, near East Grinstead, Sussex."
Apologizes for being unable to say goodbye when Caroline departed. Asks Caroline to send back the letters that she left in Jane's box. In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss Chapman, Mr. Edgersone [?], East Grinstead, Pickstone, Sussex, England."

Describes her grief at not being able to say goodbye to Caroline when she left St. Omer. Pulchérie writes that she was told to go on a walk and then ordered to Mlle. Rose's chambers. When she was finally permitted to leave, Caroline had already departed. Pulchérie speculates that the mistresses knew that she and Caroline had been writing to each other. She notes that Mlle. Rose took her key and looked in her closet; she did not find Caroline's letters, but she did find a lock of hair that Caroline had sent. She also took a book that Caroline had sent, on the grounds that it was against the rules to receive anything without permission. Mlle. Rose instructed her to write a letter to Caroline as she normally would (likely no. 12). Pulchérie is now confined to the convent du Haut-Port and instructed to "changer mon amitie pour toi," which she vows not to do. Promises to resume her correspondence after she leaves "cette maison."
Folded inside the letter is a note giving Pulchérie's address (9, Rue St. Croix Haut, St. Omer, France), and an additional fragment. In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Jane Chapman to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Caroline Chapman, Pickstone, near East Grinstead, Sussex." From London. Jane notes that she intends to keep her letter short as "I have recently answered an advertisement for a governess and am busy preparing my clothes." Describes musical performances at Exeter Hall [London], a special prayer meeting at the Poultry [London] after the return of "Mrs. Reed and Matheson" from America, and various other news of kin and friends.

Mentions Caroline's future husband: "Miss Smith, Mrs. Fisher, and Mr. C. Jones desired to be remembered to you, the latter particularl so, indeed he seems to have your name quite pat and ever and anon he brings out Caroline very tenderly, I think he is a great fop, he is very much altered since I have seen him."
Also mentions that she has written "a few lines in your letter to Pulcherie to tell her I had not her thimble."

did.unittitle: Letter from [?] Maquaire to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss Caroline Chapman… 20 Bucklesbury, Cheapside, London." Pulcherie Maquaire's sister writes on behalf of her mother, requesting that Caroline break off her correspondence with her sister. She cites "very particular reasons for forbidding this correspondence."

Passes on news that "Monesieur l'abbé Podevin" was dangerously ill but has recovered, and "Medemoiselles Podevin" are in good health "as are the mistresses." Tells Caroline not to be surprised that she has not passed along anyone's compliments, as neither the "Demoiselles Podevin" nor the "Desmoiselles Maitresses" are aware of her letter, and her mother wishes that the correspondence remain private. In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Chapman to Caroline Chapman and Jane Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "my dear children Caroline and Jane" at "Mrs. Clarke's, Ponders End" [Enfield, Middlesex, United Kingdom].
Passes along news of family and mentions that he is thinking of taking "a trip to Ponders End shortly just peep in to see you and off again."

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss C. Chapman, 20 Bucklesbury, Cheapside, London, England." From St. Pol sur Ternoise.

It seems possible that this is the first letter to pass between Caroline and Pulchérie since 1834: the address makes it appear that Pulchérie is unaware that Caroline has married; and the letter describes how Pulchérie has long "been tormented by the ardent desire to reestablish a correspondence between us." Describes her vivid memory of the day in 1834 when Caroline departed from St. Omer and describes how she was prohibited from communicating with Caroline thereafter.

Asks for assurances of Caroline's continued friendship, and gives news of what has happened since her last letter. Pulchérie is no longer with the "Dame Podevin" at St. Omer; she left in 1835 and helped to establish a pension at St. Pol sur Ternoise (approx. 60 km from St. Omer).
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Carrie Jones to [?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Opens "My dear Teacher," and closes "Yr affec Scholar Caroline Jones." Describes spiritual anxieties.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England." From St. Pol [sur Ternoise, Nord Pas de Calais, France]. Pulchérie describes her joy at receiving Caroline's letter, and begs forgiveness for her long silence. Explains that Caroline's previous letter had caused surprise and disappointment, though not displeasure. Begs for a detailed letter.

Gives news of the pension at St. Pol, which "goes as well as we could desire in a city like this one." Promises to embroider a bonnet for [Caroline's?] child, "which must be born in the month of August."
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England." From Blangy [Upper-Normandy, France]. Pulchérie writes that she is sending Caroline a box from St. Omer, containing a bonnet for Caroline's child, a ring and a collar for Caroline, and a purse for Caroline's husband. Expresses anxiety over Caroline's husband's illness and joy at Caroline's sister Helen's marriage. Forgives Caroline for her long delay in writing, but remarks that it has caused her a "very palpable suffering."
Pulchérie notes that she is on vacation ("un voyage d'agrément") in Blangy with her aunt, Natalie, and two sous-maitresses. They are staying with Pulchérie's uncle, who is the curé there.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maqauire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Squaire, London, England." Pulchérie chides Caroline for declaring herself unworthy of Pulchérie's kindness. Thanks Caroline for the ring she sent, but declares her letter too short. Tells Caroline that she might write in English if writing in French takes too much time.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England." From St. Pol, [France]. Chides Caroline for failing to write, noting that her last letter was dated 16 October [1843], and worries that Caroline has taken offense at something she has written. Describes the torment of waiting so long for a reply.

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones to [Thomas] Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "my dear Grandpapa." Announces, "I am six years old today and this is my first letter."

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England." From St. Pol. Describes her anxiety at seeing Caroline's last letter, which was written on mourning paper and contained news of the death of Caroline's child. Remarks that Caroline had seemed somewhat cold in her letters, and her subsequent silence had caused Pulchérie to worry that Caroline felt indifferent to her. Promises to send slippers for Caroline and her children. Urges Caroline to write back soon.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England." From St. Pol. Remarks on her relief at learning that Caroline's silence can be attributed to her having not received Pulchérie's last letter, rather than to any cooling of Caroline's affection. Insists that Caroline write her letters in English rather than in French, which she hopes will make maintaining their correspondence a less daunting task.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell St., Falcon Square, London." From St. Pol. Bemoans the degree to which the children at the pension are spoiled by their parents; urges Caroline to be strict with her children. Describes a recent visit to St. Omer, with much news, often delivered in a moralizing tone, of former pensionnaires and mistresses: their marriages, their children, their deaths.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell St., Falcon Square, London." From St. Pol. Chides Caroline for writing insufficiently detailed letters. Complains that Caroline tells her nothing about her growing family, and insists that she has nothing of interest to say about her own disagreeable life as mistress of a pension. Describes her dissatisfaction with Caroline's observations on religion. Concludes with reflections on Caroline's pregnancy, hoping that she remains in good health and that she might have a daughter who might replace the one Caroline had lost.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England. Describes the "torment" she has endured in the months since Caroline's last letter, which she received on 13 Jan. Begs forgiveness for the harsh words in the last letter. Offers prayers for Caroline's safe delivery, expected this month or next, and describes the bonnet she is making for the new baby.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England." Offers effusive thanks for Caroline's letter. Wishes that she could go to visit Caroline in England, but remarks regretfully that her mother will never allow her to make the trip, being too fearful of sea travel. Urges Caroline to come to France instead.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England." Explains that she has not written recently because she has been trying to finish the bonnet she promised Caroline's new child before beginning another letter. Describes a recent visit to St. Omer, and provides news of old schoolmates and mistresses, who, she claims, disapproved of the way that Pulchérie was treated at the pension when her relationship with Caroline came to light. Expresses her hope that Caroline will send her daughter Carrie to Pulchérie's pension. Notes that those who know her in St. Pol have urged her to write to her English friend to ask that she recruit English students, who would lend "un certain ton" to the pension.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from [Pulchérie Maquaire] to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Mrs. C. Jones, 36 Monkwell Street, Falcon Square, London, England." Assures Caroline of her continued affection, despite her prolonged silence. Describes the dismissal of a well-liked sous-maitresse at the pension, who had been involved in a romantic dalliance with a clerk at the sub-prefecture. Notes that the sister of an old acquaintance has gone mad, and that her parents have committed her to a "maison de santé." Attributes the epidemic of madness in France to the effect of "un enthousiasme religieux," but urges Caroline not to repeat this assessment in England, where she fears that Catholicism would be held as the culprit.
In French.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes her anguish at not receiving a letter for so long, and offers condolences on Caroline's father's death. Describes her own father, who died when Pulchérie was 3 years old, and describes the consequences of his premature death for her mother, who was from a wealthy family of "fermiers," which suffered badly in the Revolution. Passes along news from her pension in St. Pol.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Wishes his father a happy birthday, and hopes that he "will soon have more business and that we shall all soon go in the country." Requests wood to build a model cart.

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [jr] to Caroline Jones and Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Thanks his parents for the birthday gift. Describes his stay in Islington with his grandmother.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [sr] and [Carry?] Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The letter to Charles Jones sr. asks for news of family and friends in London, and describes a visit to the railway station and to the ruins of a Catholic church "called the Litten or Holy Ghost Chapel," which suggests that Thomas is writing from Basingstoke [Hampshire, England]. The second letter opens "Dear Sister," and advises the recipient to ask "aunt" to provide instruction in knitting. Remarks that he went to visit the tomb of various ancestors and saw a "grave of a girl 8 years old, just your age, and so ou might die now as well as she did when she was as old as you." This would date the letter to ca. 1849 if Carry were the recipient, or ca. 1858 if Mary Ann were the recipient. The childish hand suggests the former interpretation.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inquires about Thomas's health: "I particularly wish to know if your cough is better or worse, what sort of an appetite you have, and whether I shall send you any more of your favorite 'Ol. Jecoris aselli" [i.e., cod liver oil]." Urges him to careful religious observance: "I should like you to think about the first line in Janet's Hymn [see item TK] and ask yourself if you have done as she did, and not only have done but do you daily do it." Thomas is staying with an aunt in Basingstoke [Hampshire, England] for health reasons.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones and Jones, Charles [sr] to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On mourning paper. Caroline's letter delivers news of the baby's [Mary Ann's] illness and of work on the house. Charles sr.'s letter assigns a religious exercise: "If you want a little Sabbath employment we are going through the 53rd of Isaiah, comparing with it the gospel narrations that relate the fulfillment of the prophecies it contains, will you send me a list of those New Test. texts which are apposite."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Notes that she was "much pleased" with Thomas's letter but "should like to see an improvement in the writing, it is too small and not very legible." Remarks that the doctor says that Thomas should stay in Basingstoke as long as possible, and expresses further concerns over Thomas's health.

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [jr] to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Miss Wilkins, Basingstoke, Hants." From 36 Monkwell St. Describes the recent missionary meeting, in which Mr. Bateman (likely Rowland Bateman of the Church Missionary Society) described his work in India. Includes sketches of the objects (a fishing hook, headdresses, and clothing) that Bateman displayed. Includes family news and news of happenings at school.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes renovations of the house on Monkwell Street; passes along news of the other children's health and activities; describes Thomas's grandfather's declining health.

did.unittitle: Letter from Unidentified sender and Charles Jones [jr] to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letter on recto is unsigned; likely written by one of the other children. Describes schoolwork and passes on family news. Charles's letter describes his schoolwork, announces that "there will be elephants in the Lord Mayor's Show," and passes along news of their grandfather's rapidly failing health.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes Thomas's grandfather's declining health and death. Notes that Thomas's grandfather will be buried at Abney Park. Uses the occasion to remind Thomas about the importance of faith and the need to be prepared for sudden and unexpected death.

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [jr] to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports the death of their grandfather. Describes Guy Fawkes Day festivities.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes the ongoing work on the house, including Thomas's new bedroom. Gives a brief account of Thomas's grandfather's funeral. Expresses hope that she will be able to fetch Thomas from his aunt's in the coming week.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Promises to pick Thomas up on Tuesday. Notes progress of renovations on the house: "We are now in our new kitchen, dear Father has just been putting some of the handles on the drawers."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Stationery bears address: 138 Aldersgate St., E.C. Instructs Thomas to come home tomorrow or Wednesday, as his father has requested. Notes that typhus fever has been "prevalent" in Basingstoke.

did.unittitle: Letter from Pulchérie Maquaire to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Expresses joy at receiving her first letter from Caroline in almost four tears. Describes anguish and anxiety of waiting for news from her friend—an anxiety exacerbated by news of the ravages of cholera. Sorrowfully turns down Caroline's invitation to visit, citing her mother's fear of trains and her insistence that her daughters not travel on them. Assures Caroline that she will one day come to visit her.

did.unittitle: Letter from C[atherine] Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 6 Beech Grove, Leeds. Expresses thanks for Tom's letter and confirms that her "health is indeed greatly improved." Wishes that Tom were "as well as I am," and hopes his cough subsides when the weather warms up. Sympathizes with Tom about his schoolmaster, a "cross old man."

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [jr] to Caroline Jones and Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Crescent Irwin Street [London, England]. Informs his parents that the "Examination is fixed for Thursday Evening the 19th" and hopes that they will be present.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones and Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 10 ½ Crescent Irwin Street [London, England]. Opens with brief remarks on the upcoming examinations. Hopes to be permitted a trip to the country during the upcoming holidays.

did.unittitle: Letter from Carrie Jones to Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to 36 Monkwell St., Falcon Square, City. Describes activities in Hersham. Notes that "Baby [Mary Ann] seems to be getting more and more merry."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Jemima Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Hersham, Surrey. Remarks on her regret "that so many days and weeks should pass without a sight of my beloved Mother." Describes their living quarters at Hersham. Remarks that the baby [Mary Ann?]'s health has improved rapidly.

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [jr] to Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Hersham, [Surrey, England]. Hopes that his father will be able to visit again for "the Sunday scholar's heat." Describes Sunday's sermon and local happenings in Hersham, including the marriage of the butcher and local cricket results.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Hersham [Surrey, England]. Describes Sunday's sermon and offers further details on the butcher's wedding.

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones[jr] to Unidentified Recipient

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 36 Monkwell St. Opens "My dear Aunt." Declines an invitation to come to her house on Wednesday; asks that she propose another day.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 36 Monkwell St. A letter to Thomas on his twelfth birthday. Caroline remarks that she has "had many fears at different periods of your life that you would be taken from us before many summer terms upon your path, but you are still spared to us." Gives Thomas a box of wax given to her by her father. Hopes he will not value it less because of its age.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Thanks his mother for the gift she gave him "this morning." Notes that he will not value it any less because of its age, but "shall value it so much the more." Hopes to grow up to be a "useful man."

did.unittitle: Letter from Jemima Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 2 Sunderland Villas, Dartmouth Parks, Forest Hill. Wishes her mother "many happy returns" on her birthday.

did.unittitle: Letter from [Catherine] Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Hopes Tom's "absess (sic) is better" and "that your Mama is preparing you to go to school at the seaside after midsummer." Remarks that time in the countryside would be "beneficial" for all the children, and hopes that Tom's parents will send them out of the city as often as possible.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Describes his heart troubles and the pains in the lower part of his body. Gives an account of the Davis family, with whom he is living, and of their house and the city. The Davis's "style of living," as he describes it, is "somewhat above ours." Describes playing chess with the elder Davis daughter, Sarah, who "beats me before I know what I am about."

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [jr] to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 36 Monkwell St. Describes the Sunday morning and evening sermons at some length. Passes along news of the family business and other news of family and friends. Inquires about Thomas's life in the country: "Please write to me soon & tell me how you employ your time what time you get up & go to bed &c &c. & tell me whether you have fished yet if so how many you have caught whether you have tried in freshwater or saltwater & please if you can give some description of that part of the coast, what sort of a dog you have for a companion & please tell me what bait you use and all about your fishing."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 36 Monkwell St. Notes that it has now been a week since Thomas's departure and that "we earnestly hope & pray that the purpose of this separation may be answered by the establishment of your health & strength."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Describes how the pains in his lower body come "especially after walking, if I sit down & put my legs close together, not crossed." Asks his father to send a congregational hymn book, which is what they use at the Croft chapel in Hastings. Notes that Sarah plays the organ and leads the singing at the chapel: "she has a beautiful voice, clear & powerful but not masculine."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Reports that the pains in the lower part of his body persist, and wishes "Dr. Saunders would say something more definite than he does, I like to know all about it." Adds that he is "very happy, & among lively, intelligent, rather witty, but I believe Christian people."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Declares that he was pleased with the news he received on Jun 3, "more especially because it announced the birth of a brother." Inquires about the health of his mother and the baby, and describes his own, apparently improving, health. Reports that Mr. Davis has left Hastings to see a son feared to be ill with "brain fever."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Reports that he feels "quite strong," despite pains, palpitations, and difficulty breathing when he exerts himself. Offers his opinion on a name for the baby. Declares that he does not wish to come home yet and would prefer to stay another three or four months: "I am as happy as possible, I have now several companions, which I am very glad of."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Proposes that the baby be named Frederick. Offers a detailed account of his own health. Asks that his father send bottles of wine and oil, a sponge, and various books, including his mother's French grammar.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [jr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Gloucester Place, Hastings. Admonishes his older brother on his [sixteenth] birthday: "You are now getting gold enough to leave off some childish things which you still practice. I do wish you would leave off playing in the shop, you know it is very wrong and you have been told so many times of it." Notes that he himself "never can like [his father's business]," and that even if he did he "should not think it at all prudent or right to follow it as I think it would be at the cost of my health and eventually of my life." Asks Charles for an account of how he spent his Whit Monday.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Gloucester Place, Hastings. Notes that does not like the name Daniel for the baby, "especially with Jones, for it makes it completely Welsh at once." Asks that various items be sent to him, including books and a cricket ball.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Reports that his health is improving: the pains in the lower part of his body are now infrequent, and he only rarely suffers palpitations. Describes activities at Hastings. Reminds his mother to send the cricket ball.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Asks for a new pair of grey trousers to replace the ones he has, which have a hole in the knee. Notes that he is working on preparations for a "fancy sale," which is to take place in August and to benefit "the girls British school." Enclosed is a pencil sketch of Croft Chapel, Hastings, done by Thomas and dated 1854 May 18.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Remarks, as he already has to his brother, that he "never can like your business… nor do I think it will at all suit me." Mentions that he has "very often thought of profession but have also thought that I am not fit for that yet." Describes how he spends his sabbaths and what he is reading.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Asks his father to send material for a machine for cutting and polishing pebbles, as he has "found some truly beautiful stones."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, [Hastings]. Reports that he has received the new trousers. Notes that his "heart still troubles [him] occasionally with both pains & palpitation" and that he still has "the pains in my lower part of my body, occasionally."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones Sr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Wishes his father a happy birthday. Complains that he has not had a letter since 10 July, and that his mother has not sent one since 5 July. Describes his recent expenses and asks for postage stamps.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Notes that he is sorry to hear that his sisters have been unwell: "I wish Carry and all of you could get out of that horrid metropolis for a few weeks." Describes activities in Hastings: "I do not find much time to study but I trust I improve my singing by constant company with such an accomplished musician & beautiful singer as Sarah."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Reports on his health, his activities in Hastings, and the doings of the Davis family.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Notes that pains in the lower part of his body are much the same, but that his heart hardly troubles him at all. Adds that he is not ready to return home yet: "I should undoubtedly be very happy to see you but still I should like to stay here a little longer."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Passes on family news, and notifies Thomas of a planned family trip to Ramsgate [Kent, England]. Reports that Carry is recovering from an illness which "began with diarrhea," and notes that "the cholera has been rather prevalent in London, but I hope is on the decrease." Includes health advice and much religious admonition.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Remarks that he is glad to hear about the plans to go to Ramsgate, but thinks his mother "ought to have someone with you to take care of the children, or I am afraid you will not get much benefit yourself, it will be such a constant work & care for you & Carry." Hopes to stay in Hastings until at least 11 September, to see the Hastings regatta. Notes that "you did not tell me before what was the matter with Carry, I suppose you were afraid of frightening me." Adds that there have been very few cases of cholera in Hastings, and that the parish where he lives has had a particularly low death rate in recent years.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [Sr] and Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. The letter to Charles sr. opens with a discussion of the ethics or cricket-playing; reports that he is still "very much troubled" by the wandering of his mind during prayer; offers further thoughts on the possibility of entering the ministry. The letter to Caroline describes the gifts Mrs. Davis is sending to the Jones family out of the items left over after the fancy sale.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 36 Monkwell St. Describes trip to Ramsgate, cut short because Thomas's sister Jemima was "so ill with diarrhea that I was anxious about her." Notes that "the cholera is very prevalent at Ramsgate."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [Jr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Describes his visit to Bodiam Castle, during which one of his traveling companions accidentally set the castle on fire when lighting his pipe.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Notes that he hears "very bad accounts indeed of the health of London" but also hears that cholera is on the decrease. Describes meeting an old acquaintance, reports on his health, and gives a brief account of the regatta in Hastings.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports on Jemima's illness, which the doctor terms "simple fever." Notes that she "is very thin & weak as a little infant." Prays "that the same kind & gracious God who healed your sickness will restore to us this precious lamb of our little flock."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Responds to news of Jemima's illness.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones and Charles Jones [sr] to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports on Jemima's illness: "I scarcely know wether to say dear Jemima is better or not." Asks Thomas whether he would like to come home and see her, since she is in "a very precarious state." Brief note from Charles Jones sr. at foot of final page.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Remarks that he still lives "in prayer and hope," taking comfort in the fact that when Sarah Davis was a small child she "had just the same disease; for a long time they did not expect her to live; for one week she took nothing but cold water, & was wasted away to nothing almost." Would like to see Jemima, but thinks it best not to come home unless her health worsens: he "could do no good" in London, and thinks "it would be a great disadvantage… to come directly out of this pure air into a house where fever is."

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones[Jr] and Charles Jones [Sr] to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 36 Monkwell St. Charles jr. describes the recovery of "dear little Jemima," one of the youngest of the Jones children: "Today she tries to speak a little, for she has not spoken in many days, she is very fretful, which is considered a good sign but not a pleasant one." Further news of the health of family and friends. On the back, a brief note from Charles sr., expressing his happiness that Thomas has taken "the right view in Jemima's case."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [Jr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Hastings. Remarks on his "relief" at learning that "dear little Jemima was better." Notes that he has seen an account of Mr. Spence's recognition services [at Poultry Chapel?] in the Patriot. Accuses his brother of "laboring under the effects of a queer delusion as regards your having written to me last," and hopes that "perhaps in future you will follow my example & date your letters, then I could tell by reference."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports that Carry has become ill and that Charley has caught a cold, but that Jemima's health is improving. Notes that the doctor says "he has an immense number of fever cases just now."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Notes that he thought that something must have been wrong because he had not received any letters recently. Remarks that Mrs. Davies had always suspected that the fever "would run through the family." Worries that Charley's cold might turn into a fever.

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [Jr] to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports on health of his siblings: notes that Carry "seems to be getting better, although it is slowly," while Jemima "is mostly sitting up" and "ate heartily of a leg of mutton." Meanwhile, "Baby" [Henry Chapman] is fat and healthy: "he is a fine specimen & does honor to his sex." Reports that he himself is "getting better" though "the doctor said I had better keep quiet."
Describes the "dreadful battle of Alma."

Describes how he misses his brother: "I hope not many weeks will elapse before you return. There is a great draught all down my back at night because you are not there to fill up the cavity or vacancy."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to [Charles Jones Jr.]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Reports that he was "very glad to hear that you were better; and also about Carry and Jemima." Requests a fuller account of the recognition service for Mr. Spence. Wishes he had a chess partner.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports that Charley is now ill with the fever as well, though Carry and Jemima are improving. Recommends Hebrews 12 as comfort for the afflicted and urges Thomas to dress warmly. Encloses "a little sketch of dear Charley's taken from the Leisure Hour while he was poorly up stairs, representing Frank Layton's encounter with an enraged bull."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Worries over his mother's health; wishes she could find someone to help; asks about the health of Charley and the other Jones children; describes his doings in Hastings.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Charles Jones [Sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Richmond. Inquires about Charles's plans to join the family in Richmond; asks him to bring various items, including "some tracts," requested "by a poor old woman"; and asks him to have the tea-pot repaired they return.

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [Sr] to Charles Jones [Jr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Assures Charley that his forgiveness will always be forthcoming "when sincerely sought."

did.unittitle: Letter from Charles Jones [Jr] to [Grandma]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Thanks his grandmother for the drops she sent, which "did my cough much good." Describes his schedule and curriculum at school.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Charles Jones [Sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Reacts to news of Charley's death: "I really cannot realise the fact that my dear healthy brother that I left at home is dead, I do not think I shall quite realise it until I come home." Wishes he could attend his brother's funeral.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Attempts to comfort Thomas following the death of his brother Charley. Lists the friends and relatives who have come "to console with us." Notes that the family is reserving for Thomas "the mournful pleasure of looking over your dear brother's lock up drawer, cabinet & treasure box" and that Thomas is to have Charley's reference Bible and watch guard.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Looks forward to his return home but also dreads it, "for I shall then feel the affliction more than I do now, it will be so strange, I shall feel so desolate & so lonely." Hopes that his mother, father, and Carry "do not indulge in overmuch sorrow." Worries about Carry's health.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes Charley's funeral. Reports that Carry and Jemima are improving, but that Thomas's school-fellow Wyatt has died, having been taken ill with diarrhea and "an inward fever."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: From 1 Gloucester Place, Hastings. Expresses gratitude for the account of Charley's funeral. Describes his continued inability to imagine home without his older brother. Reacts to the news of his sisters' improving health and to the news of deaths in the families of relatives and friends.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reports that Bundy, who worked in Charles Jones's shop, is ill with fever and has been sent to the "Fever Hospital" on Liverpool Road. Remarks that Charles's father and grandfather have been in to visit him, and notes that "your father is of course very put out about losing the two boys [sc. Charley and Bundy] as he has to pump water, sweep shop &c, but he will soon I hope get another lad." Notes that they sent Bundy to the hospital in part because they worried that further illness in the house would delay Thomas's return home, and in part because they "were just going to have whitewashers & plasterers in."

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Remarks that he was sorry to hear that Bundy was ill but hoped that "this affliction may be blessed to his conversion for he has at present lived a very wicked life, much more so I think than you can imagine, for I of course have heard and seen much more of it in the shop than you can have." Notes that he is enclosing a letter to Bundy and asks his mother to find him a copy of the "Sinner's Friend" to give him with the letter.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Notes that Thomas's father has gone to see Bundy and taken him Thomas's letter. Anticipates Thomas's return eagerly: "Your Mother longs to embrace you, now her eldest child & one to whom she looks though tremblingly for comfort & sweet intercourse."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Informs Thomas that he is to come home on Thursday.

did.unittitle: Letter from Thomas Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes travel plans for his return to London.

did.unittitle: Jones Henry to [unidentified recipient]
Letter from Henry Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Ilford [Essex, England]. Notes that Caroline told him "to give your love to cousins, dog and pigeons. I did to my cousins, but the dog and pigeons must take it themselves." Describes a recent thunderstorm, and gives an account of other activities in Ilford. Asks his mother to tell him in her next letter "if the war on the Continent has really begun."

did.unittitle: Letter from Mary Ann Jones to [Carry?] Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Opens "my dear sister. I was glad to have your letter. I read it myself."

did.unittitle: Letter from Carrie Jones to Charles Jones [Sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Discusses her religious concerns. Asserts that she wishes "to be joined to the Church of Christ" and that she is "going to try to do all I can for the Jews also heathen (sic)."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Thomas Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Admonishes Thomas for failing to write.

did.unittitle: Letter from [Caroline Jones] to [Charles Jones Sr.]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Opens, "My dearest one." Remarks that she has "thought very much of our precious tom since I left home." Describes how the rail journey [to Hastings] reminded her of the "journey of life, sometimes the sun shines brightly upon us & all is pleasant & gay, and then suddenly we are plunged into darkness, but there was light provided within that it was not all dark… and I thought our darling boy was not afraid, and I believe he would have said if he could 'thy rod & thy staff they comfort me.'" Mentions visiting Croft Chapel and Mr. Davis's tombstone.

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Charles Jones [Sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Hastings. Written on the eve of their twenty-fourth anniversary. Describes her living situation; gives an account of sea bathing. Hopes Charles will be able to join them in Richmond.

did.unittitle: Letter from Emma Jones to "Miss Pound"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A poem titled "The Falun (sic) Bird." Closes, "to Miss Pound, From Little Emma."

did.unittitle: Letter from Caroline Jones to Charles Jones [Sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Laurel [?] Cottage, Clifton St. Notes that the next day will mark 28 years since Charles first wrote to her and reminisces about their marriage. Inquires about when Charles will be able to join the rest of the family at Laurel Cottage and briefly describes their living situation there.

did.unittitle: Letter from Emma Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Describes arrival in Seaton [Devon] and living situation there, where she is staying with two of her aunts.

did.unittitle: Letter from Emma Jones to Charles Jones [Sr]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Glayston Cottage. Wishes her father a happy birthday. Describes playing cricket and other activities at Glayston Cottage, where she is staying with her aunt Jane.

did.unittitle: Letter from Emma Jones to Caroline Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 138 Aldersgate St. [London]. Addressed to "Dearest very Dearest Mama." Describes receiving a letter from her sister Carrie, who had expressed concern that Emma "thought that her letters were all about Jesus & prayer but she said she had had prayer answered so many times that she could not help talking about it." Describes the gifts she has received from her father, siblings, and other relatives.

did.unittitle: Letter from Emma Jones to Caroline Jones
Letter from Henry Chapman Jones to [Emma Jones]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In envelope addressed to Miss E.L. Jones, 166 Blackstock Road, Highbury, N. London. From Mrs. Fry, Marlborough Villa Road, Ventnor, I.W. Opens "My dear Toddie" and closes "Your affectionate Dod Henry." Describes travels on the Isle of Wight.

did.unittitle: Series III. Sydney Langford Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series includes Sydney Langford Jones's school exercise books, complete with the caustic comments of schoolmasters; and his Royal College of Art notebooks, with marginalia of witty caricatures and decorations showing architectural details. There are also many of his tiny engravings, and some of his design work. Also present are photographs of him at work, and a rare copy of his Peace portfolio, produced with Laurence Housman (1935).

Series IV. Arthur Morley Jones

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material in this series reflect the life of Arthur Morley Jones, especially relating to his childhood in the 1910s. A sketchbook from later in his life is also included.

Series V. Other Jones family members

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material in this series comes from other members of the Jones family. It includes household books written or or owned by women in the family (but whose first names are not given), as well as a travel diary belonging to Mary Anne Jones (1850-1926). Almanacs are also present, as well as a record of marks and students in Mr. Jones' art room.

did.unittitle: Series VI. Photographs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The photograph series contains two large oversized photographs on board, a carte de visite album, and the contents a photograph album that was disbound. The contents of the photograph album contain both studio and family portraits, scenery of various parts of England, and ephemera including hand drawn cards for Christmas and several printed woodcuts, the latter likely by Sydney Langford Jones. A menu in this section from a dinner event also contains the signiatures of Robert Baden-Powell and Ernest Shackleton.
Most photographers in this series are unidentified and it should not be assumed that Henry Chapman Jones took photographs unless given specific attribution.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Material in this series is arranged alphabetically.

Unbound album

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This material was originally housed in a brown leather photograph album. In addition to photographs is contains hand drawn cards by both Sydney Langford and Arthur Morley, as well as examples of woodcuts likely done by Sydney. Also present are pieces of ephemera such as menus and postcards.
